name: Alpe d'Huez & the Grands Cols — Bourg-d'Oisans
tagline: Ride the 21 hairpins, the Galibier and the Croix de Fer from the little valley town where every Tour de France legend has signed on.
dates: Riding season: mid-June – late September (high cols hold snow into early June and can close again from mid-October). Col du Galibier summit road opened ~5 June 2026 and typically reopens the first week of June 2027 (expected); Croix de Fer and Lautaret open earlier, late May. Marmotte Granfondo Alpes: the 2026 edition ran Sunday 28 June; the 2027 edition is expected late June / early July 2027, with registration opening early October 2026 (expected) — its 7,500 places sell out within days. Note: the 2026 Tour de France finished atop Alpe d'Huez twice (24–25 July 2026), so expect residual Tour fever — and check the 2027 route (announced October 2026) before planning a July 2027 trip, as Tour week brings road closures and full hotels.
history: Alpe d'Huez earned its place in cycling's liturgy on 4 July 1952, when the Tour de France staged its first-ever summit finish there and Fausto Coppi rode away to win it. The climb itself is almost geometrically perfect: 13.8 km at 8.1% average from Bourg-d'Oisans at about 720 m to the resort at 1,850 m, folded into 21 numbered hairpins that count down from 21 at the bottom to 1 at the top. Each bend carries the name of a stage winner, starting with Coppi; when the winners outnumbered the bends, the organisers simply started around again. The steepest ramps — over 10% — come brutally early, through bends 21 to 16, before the gradient settles into its relentless rhythm. Marco Pantani's 1997 ascent of roughly 37 and a half minutes remains the benchmark no one has touched.

The Dutch adopted the mountain outright: Dutch riders won eight of the first fourteen Tour finishes on the Alpe, earning it the nickname 'the Dutch Mountain', and bend 7 — by the little church of Saint-Ferréol — became 'Dutch Corner', an orange-smoke amphitheatre of 100,000-strong crowds on Tour days. But the Alpe is only the front door to the Oisans. Above the Romanche valley rise the other cathedrals: the Col du Galibier (2,642 m), first crossed by the Tour in 1911 and so beloved by founder Henri Desgrange that his memorial stands near its summit — he wrote that beside the Galibier, all other climbs were 'gnat-soup'; the long, wild Col de la Croix de Fer (2,067 m) with its iron summit cross and views to the Aiguilles d'Arves; and the Col du Lautaret (2,058 m), the great trade pass beneath the glaciers of La Meije.

Bourg-d'Oisans, the plain little market town on the valley floor, is the sport's most efficient base camp: five legendary climbs start within riding distance of its boulangeries. It is also the birthplace of the modern sportive — La Marmotte, first run in 1982 and generally counted the oldest and most prestigious cyclosportive in the world. Its 177 km route over the Glandon/Croix de Fer, Télégraphe and Galibier, finishing up the 21 bends, packs in some 5,000 m of climbing and draws 7,500 riders from across the globe every summer. The 2026 Tour de France sealed the region's status with an unprecedented double: back-to-back stage finishes on the Alpe, the second — from Bourg-d'Oisans over the Croix de Fer, Télégraphe, Galibier and the rough-backed Col de Sarenne — a virtual Marmotte raced by the pros.
whyGo: Nowhere else in cycling can you sleep in one unpretentious valley town and tick off this density of Tour de France monuments: the 21 hairpins before breakfast, the Galibier as a full pilgrimage day, the Croix de Fer as the connoisseur's epic. For a Zürich-based rider it is the closest of the truly mythic French climbs — a five-to-six-hour drive or a manageable rail journey via Geneva — which makes even a long weekend viable in September, and a Marmotte campaign in summer 2027 the natural season goal. The riding is honest, the road surfaces superb, the col-top coffee ritual perfected over a century.

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Bern → Geneva → Annecy/Chambéry → Grenoble → D1091 up the Romanche valley to Bourg-d'Oisans
gettingThere.cost: ~CHF 90–130 each way in fuel + French autoroute tolls (~€35–45); Swiss vignette required
gettingThere.notes: The default for cyclists — your own bike, wheels and gearing, plus freedom to relocate a day to Valloire or the Lautaret. The final 50 km from Grenoble up the gorge is slow but scenic; avoid Saturday changeover traffic in July–August.

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Geneva (IC, ~2h45) → Grenoble (TER or FlixBus, ~2h) → Cars Région Isère bus T87 to Bourg-d'Oisans (~1h15)
gettingThere.cost: ~CHF 70–140 each way with advance fares; T87 bus a few euros
gettingThere.notes: Entirely doable with a bike: bikes travel free or cheaply on the Geneva–Grenoble TER (reserve in summer), and the T87 takes bikes subject to space. Renting in Bourg-d'Oisans and travelling light is the low-stress version.

gettingThere.route: Zürich (ZRH) → Geneva or Lyon Saint-Exupéry, then hire car ~2.5–3h to Bourg-d'Oisans
gettingThere.cost: CHF 100–250 return + car hire from ~€40/day; bike box ~CHF 100 per direction
gettingThere.notes: Rarely worth it from Zürich given the drive is barely longer door-to-door — consider only if combining with another trip or if Geneva flights are cheap and you plan to rent a bike locally.

costs.thrifty: CHF 600–950 for a week: pitch at one of Bourg-d'Oisans' excellent cyclist campsites (La Cascade or Le Colporteur, ~€25–45/night for a pitch or ~€60–80 for a simple cabin), own bike brought by car or train, market and supermarket food, col-top coffees as the only luxury.
costs.midrange: CHF 1,500–2,100 for a week: 2–3* hotel or half-board logis in Bourg-d'Oisans or Le Bourg-d'Arud (~€90–140/night), quality carbon road bike rental with compact gearing (~€45–60/day, ~€250–350/week from the shops on the main street), restaurant dinners and a massage after the Galibier day.
costs.splurge: CHF 3,000–4,500+: guided cycling week with vehicle support and guaranteed Marmotte entry (packages ~€2,500–3,500 through operators like Sports Tours International), 4* comfort at Alpe d'Huez itself or a boutique chalet, top-tier rental bike and a photographer-followed ascent of the 21 bends.
costs.notes: No lift pass is needed for road cycling — the climbs are free, which is the whole point. Marmotte Granfondo Alpes entry runs roughly €100–140 depending on registration tier and sells out almost immediately; tour-operator packages guarantee a start. Budget €10–15 per col-day for refuge coffee, omelette and tarte aux myrtilles — the true local tax.

booking.label: Marmotte Granfondo Alpes — official site & registration
Bike Oisans — official cycling portal, col opening dates & routes
Alpe d'Huez tourism — official resort site

weather: July–August: the Bourg-d'Oisans valley floor regularly hits 28–33°C by noon while the Galibier summit sits at 5–12°C — you ride through three climate zones in one ascent. June and September are the sweet spots (valley 18–25°C, quieter roads), but snow can linger beside the road at 2,600 m into late June and dust the cols again from late September. Afternoon thunderstorms build fast on hot days. Pack for the summit, not the valley: gilet, arm warmers, full-finger gloves and a rain shell in the jersey pockets on every high-col day — the 45 km descent from the Galibier back to Bourg-d'Oisans is cold even in August.
tips: Register for the Marmotte 2027 the morning entries open (expected early October 2026) — 7,500 places have sold out in under a day in recent years; a tour-operator package with guaranteed entry is the fallback.

Going in June? Verify col openings first: the Galibier summit road usually opens in the first week of June (tunnel a week earlier), Croix de Fer and Lautaret by late May — check the Bike Oisans and Valloire road-status pages before committing, and enjoy the snow walls if you time it right.

Ride Alpe d'Huez before 8 am: the steepest ramps (bends 21–16, over 10%) come first with zero shade, valley heat arrives by mid-morning, and early hours mean fewer camper vans on the hairpins. Turn it into a loop by continuing over the wilder Col de Sarenne and descending the Ferrand valley instead of retracing the bends.

Be honest about the Galibier day: from Bourg-d'Oisans it is ~48 km of nearly continuous ascent — the long false-flat drag to the Lautaret, then 8.5 km at ~7% to 2,642 m, where thin air above 2,400 m bites harder than the gradient. Fit a 34x30 or easier and treat it as a full day out.

For quieter miles, use the balcony roads: the Auris and Villard-Reculas balconies and the Col d'Ornon see a fraction of the Alpe's traffic, and climbing the Croix de Fer via Vaujany and the lakes is both harder and far more beautiful than the standard D926 approach.

Be off the high cols by ~15:00 on hot days — afternoon storms are the region's real hazard — and never start a descent without layers: Galibier–Lautaret–Bourg-d'Oisans is 45+ km downhill and genuinely cold at 60 km/h.

localRecs.name: Chalet du Col de la Croix de Fer
localRecs.note: The refuge-restaurant at the 2,067 m summit — omelette, croûte au fromage and blueberry tart facing the three towers of the Aiguilles d'Arves. The classic proof-of-summit stop.

localRecs.name: Dutch Corner (Virage 7)
localRecs.note: Bend 7 of the 21, by the tiny church of Saint-Ferréol — the orange epicentre of Tour madness. On a quiet morning you can stand alone where 100,000 fans roared during the 2026 double finish.

localRecs.name: Jardin du Lautaret
localRecs.note: A century-old alpine botanical garden at 2,100 m on the Col du Lautaret, beneath the glaciers of La Meije — over 2,000 mountain plant species and the finest rest-stop excuse on the Galibier ride.

localRecs.name: Venosc
localRecs.note: Car-free artisan village up the Vénéon valley, a flat 30-minute spin from Bourg-d'Oisans — craft workshops, stone lanes and crêperies; the perfect recovery-day destination, with a gondola up to Les Deux Alpes.

localRecs.name: Pic Blanc (3,330 m)
localRecs.note: Cable car from Alpe d'Huez to a 360° platform over the Écrins glaciers — they claim a fifth of France is visible on a clear day. The rest-day ascent that requires no watts.

photos.caption: Alpe d'Huez on its sunlit balcony at 1,850 m — the 21 hairpins climb roughly 1,100 vertical metres to reach it from the Romanche valley floor.
The Col du Galibier (2,642 m) — often the highest point of the Tour de France, and the summit of the Marmotte's long day out.
The D926 winding through high pasture toward the Col de la Croix de Fer (2,067 m), the wild westerly giant of the Marmotte route.
